name: Kimi K2.7-Code
description: Moonshot AI's coding-specialized open-weight MoE (1T total / 32B active, Modified MIT) built on Kimi K2.6, released 2026-06-12. Vendor reports 62.0 on Kimi Code Bench v2 (+21.8% over K2.6) and ~30% lower reasoning-token usage, but all published benchmarks are Moonshot-run with no independent public-suite results yet. 256K context, thinking mode always on, $0.95/$4.00 per 1M tokens.

evidence.value: Kimi Code Bench v2: 62.0 vs K2.6's 50.9 (+21.8%); MCP Mark Verified 81.1 vs Claude Opus 4.8's 76.4; trails GPT-5.5 on most vendor-run metrics
Positioning is token efficiency in agentic coding rather than raw benchmark leadership; all published benchmarks are vendor-run, not independent

task_accuracy_code.methodology: Vendor-run proprietary benchmarks only (Kimi Code Bench v2, MCP Mark Verified); no public-suite (SWE-bench) results or independent replication yet — scored by anchoring to K2.6's verified base with the specialization claim discounted

evidence.value: Built on K2.6's base with mandatory thinking mode; coding specialization trades some general reasoning breadth; ~30% lower reasoning-token usage claimed at comparable quality

task_accuracy_reasoning.methodology: Inference from K2.6 lineage and vendor claims; no independent reasoning benchmarks published for K2.7-Code

evidence.value: Coding-focused specialization of K2.6; general-domain performance not a design goal and not benchmarked by the vendor

task_accuracy_general.methodology: Review of vendor positioning; general capability anchored slightly below the K2.6 generalist base

evidence.value: Sampling parameters fixed server-side and thinking mode mandatory, which promotes run-to-run consistency; only weeks of community testing so far

output_consistency.methodology: Early community testing of repeated runs and agent trajectories; limited observation window since June launch

evidence.value: Comparable per-request latency to K2.6 (32B active); ~30% fewer reasoning tokens shortens end-to-end agentic turns despite mandatory thinking mode

latency_p50.methodology: Median latency for API requests with standard prompt sizes; varies widely by host

evidence.value: p95 ~7.0s; long agentic coding chains take substantially longer by design

latency_p95.methodology: 95th percentile response time from early third-party measurements

evidence.value: 256K (262,144) token context window, unchanged from K2.6

context_window.methodology: Official specification from model card, confirmed by OpenRouter listing (262K)

evidence.value: Served on the same first-party platform as K2.6 (generally stable); open weights allow self-hosted redundancy; no long-run record for this endpoint yet

uptime.methodology: Review of platform availability and self-hosting fallback options; observation window under one month

performance_reliability.notes: Vendor claims meaningful coding gains over the already-strong K2.6 (62.0 vs 50.9 on Kimi Code Bench v2) plus ~30% lower reasoning-token usage, but every published number is Moonshot-run on proprietary benchmarks — no SWE-bench or other public-suite results exist yet. Scores anchor to K2.6's verified base with the uplift discounted until independent replication.

evidence.value: Inherits K2.6 safety tuning; no published third-party prompt-injection audit; agentic coding use (tool execution, repo access) raises injection stakes

prompt_injection_resistance.methodology: Review of vendor safety documentation and K2.6 precedent against OWASP LLM01 patterns; model too new for mature red-team coverage

evidence.value: Standard alignment tuning; open weights mean guardrails can be removed in fine-tuned derivatives; limited adversarial testing published since launch

jailbreak_resistance.methodology: Early testing against adversarial prompt datasets; open-weight deployments inherit deployer responsibility

evidence.value: Standard data handling on first-party API (same platform and policies as K2.6); full control when self-hosted

data_leakage_prevention.methodology: Analysis of privacy policies and self-hosting data-control options

evidence.value: Safety post-training inherited from K2.6; refusal behavior comparable to other open frontier models per early reports

output_safety.methodology: Safety testing across harmful content categories per vendor card and K2.6 precedent

evidence.value: API key authentication, HTTPS only, rate limiting; OpenAI-compatible endpoints shared with the K2 family

api_security.methodology: Review of API security features and best practices

security.notes: Inherits K2.6's standard open-model posture with no third-party audit. As a coding agent typically wired to tool execution and repositories, deployers should treat prompt-injection hardening as their own responsibility.

data_residency.value: China (first-party API); any jurisdiction when self-hosted or via Western hosts

evidence.value: Moonshot AI is a China-based provider; first-party API data processed under Chinese jurisdiction
Available via OpenRouter and Western inference hosts within days of release, enabling non-China residency

data_residency.methodology: Review of provider jurisdiction and third-party hosting options

evidence.value: API data usage terms standard for the segment (unchanged from K2.6); self-hosting removes the question entirely

training_data_optout.methodology: Analysis of privacy policy and data usage terms

data_retention.value: Per Moonshot policy on first-party API (China jurisdiction); zero when self-hosted

evidence.value: First-party retention governed by Chinese data regulations; self-hosted deployments retain nothing externally

data_retention.methodology: Review of terms of service and deployment-dependent retention

evidence.value: Customer responsible for PII redaction; no managed PII tooling; source code sent to the API may itself contain secrets and PII

pii_handling.methodology: Review of data protection capabilities and customer responsibilities

evidence.value: No published SOC 2 / HIPAA / GDPR attestations for the first-party API; Western hosts may carry their own certifications

compliance_certifications.methodology: Verification of compliance certifications and audit reports

evidence.value: Self-hosting via vLLM/SGLang (~595 GB on disk) gives complete data control and zero external retention — attractive for keeping proprietary code in-house

zero_data_retention.methodology: Review of self-hosting deployment options enabling zero retention

privacy_compliance.notes: Same posture as K2.6: first-party API under Chinese jurisdiction is a material caveat — amplified for this model because coding workloads routinely transmit proprietary source code. Open weights fully mitigate for organizations able to self-host or use Western hosts.

evidence.value: Thinking mode is mandatory, so reasoning traces are always available; tool-call trajectories inspectable as with K2.6's agentic stack

explainability.methodology: Evaluation of reasoning and agent-trajectory transparency

evidence.value: Early reports mirror K2.6: moderate hallucination, with tool-use grounding improving factuality in agentic coding loops

hallucination_rate.methodology: Early testing on factual QA and tool-augmented coding workflows

evidence.value: Limited published bias evaluation; lower salience for a coding-specialized model but unevaluated nonetheless

bias_fairness.methodology: Review of published bias benchmarks and community evaluations

evidence.value: Expresses uncertainty adequately in thinking traces; no calibrated confidence outputs

uncertainty_quantification.methodology: Qualitative assessment of confidence expression in outputs

evidence.value: Detailed card: 1T/32B MoE, 61 layers, 384 experts, MLA attention, 400M MoonViT vision encoder, deployment guides; benchmark section limited to Moonshot-proprietary suites

model_card_quality.methodology: Review of documentation completeness and clarity

evidence.value: Architecture well documented; the coding-specialization training recipe and data composition on top of K2.6 are not disclosed

training_data_transparency.methodology: Review of public disclosures about training data

evidence.value: Built-in safety tuning inherited from K2.6; deployers of open weights must layer their own guardrails, especially around code-execution tools

trust_transparency.notes: Open weights, a detailed architecture card, and always-on thinking traces give decent transparency, but the evaluation story is weaker than K2.6's: all benchmarks are Moonshot-proprietary (Kimi Code Bench v2, MCP Mark Verified) with no public-suite or independent results yet.

evidence.value: OpenAI-compatible API with streaming, tool calling, vision input, and prompt caching; constraint: thinking mode is mandatory and sampling parameters are fixed server-side

api_design_quality.methodology: Review of API design, consistency, and feature completeness; server-side sampling constraints reduce configurability

evidence.value: OpenAI-compatible so mainstream SDKs work; first-party Kimi Code CLI ships alongside the model for agentic coding workflows

sdk_quality.methodology: Review of SDK quality, documentation, and maintenance

evidence.value: K2.7-Code is a coding-specialized sibling built on K2.6 (released eight weeks prior), not a replacement — K2.6 remains the general-purpose flagship and prior weights stay available; cadence is fast

versioning_policy.methodology: Review of versioning practices and weight availability

evidence.value: Basic usage dashboard; self-hosted observability is deployer-built

monitoring_observability.methodology: Review of available monitoring tools and metrics

evidence.value: GitHub and community support; limited English-language enterprise support

support_quality.methodology: Assessment of documentation, community, and support responsiveness

evidence.value: Listed on OpenRouter within days ($0.74/$3.50 via routed providers) with vLLM/SGLang self-hosting support; rides the mature K2-family ecosystem but is itself weeks old

ecosystem_maturity.methodology: Analysis of third-party hosting, integrations, and tooling; conservative given launch recency

evidence.value: Same Modified MIT as K2.6: MIT with an attribution-UI requirement for deployments exceeding 100M MAU or $20M/month revenue

license_terms.methodology: Review of licensing terms and restrictions; attribution clause is trust-relevant for large-scale commercial use

operational_excellence.notes: Inherits the K2 family's strong ecosystem (OpenRouter within days, Kimi Code CLI, vLLM/SGLang). Operational quirks to note: thinking mode cannot be disabled and sampling parameters are fixed server-side, which constrains tuning; the ~30% token-usage reduction partly offsets thinking-mode cost.

code-generation.notes: Purpose-built for agentic coding on the strong K2.6 base with ~30% lower token usage; vendor claims large gains (62.0 Kimi Code Bench v2, 81.1 MCP Mark Verified vs Opus 4.8's 76.4) but all benchmarks are Moonshot-run — verify on your own workloads.

customer-support.notes: Coding-specialized with mandatory thinking mode — poorly matched to simple support flows.

content-creation.notes: Not its purpose; the general-purpose K2.6 is the better Moonshot pick for prose.

data-analysis.notes: Strong for code-heavy analysis pipelines (notebooks, ETL, tooling); K2.6 better for broad analytical reasoning.

research-assistant.notes: Capable tool-use and 256K context, but reasoning breadth trades toward code; use K2.6 for general research.

legal-compliance.notes: Wrong specialization, China-jurisdiction first-party API, and no Western certifications.

healthcare.notes: Not recommended: coding-specialized and no compliant first-party path; health-software engineering should still self-host.

financial-analysis.notes: Good for quant-developer workflows (code-first); data residency requires self-hosting for regulated firms.

education.notes: Strong coding tutor with visible thinking traces; less suited to general STEM tutoring than K2.6 or GLM-5.

creative-writing.notes: Coding specialization comes at the cost of prose quality; pick a generalist.

strengths: Coding-specialized on the strong K2.6 base: vendor reports 62.0 Kimi Code Bench v2 (+21.8% over K2.6) and 81.1 MCP Mark Verified (vs Claude Opus 4.8's 76.4)
~30% lower reasoning-token usage than K2.6, cutting cost in agentic loops where thinking bills as output
Open weights under Modified MIT with full self-hosting (vLLM/SGLang) for keeping proprietary code in-house
Always-on thinking traces aid debugging and auditability of agent runs
256K context with text and image input (400M MoonViT encoder) for UI-aware coding
Broad availability within days: Kimi API, Kimi Code CLI, Hugging Face, OpenRouter ($0.74/$3.50 routed)
limitations: All published benchmarks are Moonshot-proprietary and vendor-run — no SWE-bench or independent public-suite results yet
Thinking mode cannot be disabled and sampling parameters are fixed server-side, limiting tuning
First-party Moonshot API processes data (including submitted source code) under Chinese jurisdiction with limited Western certifications
Modified MIT license imposes attribution-UI requirement above 100M MAU or $20M/month revenue
Coding specialization narrows general-purpose capability vs K2.6
Self-hosting a 1T-parameter MoE (~595 GB on disk) requires substantial GPU infrastructure
Weeks old: consistency, uptime, and security evidence still immature
best_for: Long-horizon agentic coding where reasoning-token spend dominates cost
Teams already on K2.6 or Kimi Code wanting a cheaper-per-task coding specialist
Self-hosted coding assistants that must keep proprietary source code in-house
Multi-turn repository engineering within a 256K context
not_recommended_for: Teams requiring independently verified benchmarks before adoption (vendor-only so far)
General-purpose or creative workloads — K2.6 or a generalist model fits better
Regulated Western workloads sending source code to the first-party API
Deployments needing custom sampling parameters or thinking-mode control
Hyperscale consumer products unwilling to satisfy the attribution-UI license clause

pricing.notes: First-party Moonshot API pricing at launch, same headline rates as K2.6; mandatory thinking mode bills as output, partly offset by ~30% lower token usage. OpenRouter routed pricing ~$0.74/$3.50.

metadata.license: Modified MIT (attribution-UI requirement above 100M MAU or $20M/month revenue)
metadata.architecture: Mixture-of-Experts built on Kimi K2.6: 1T total / 32B active parameters, 61 layers, 384 experts (8 selected + 1 shared), MLA attention, SwiGLU, 400M MoonViT vision encoder; coding-specialized post-training with mandatory thinking mode
